coredata.dc:title: Water-soluble graphene quantum dot‐based polymer nanoparticles with internal donor/acceptor heterojunctions for efficient and selective detection of cancer cells
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Chang, Yi-Hsuan
Chiang, Wei-Hung
Ilhami, Fasih Bintang
Tsai, Cheng-Yu
Huang, Sin-Yu
Cheng, Chih-Chia
coredata.dc:description: We present a new, insightful donor–acceptor (D-A) energy transfer-based strategy for the preparation and development of water-soluble multifunctional pH-responsive heterojunction nanoparticles. Hydrophilic tertiary amine-grafted polythiophene (WPT) as a donor and blue fluorescent graphene quantum dots (GQD) as an acceptor spontaneously form co-assembled nanoparticles that function as a highly pH-sensitive and efficient biosensor appropriate for the detection of cancer cells. These WPT/GQD nanoparticles exhibit a number of unique physical characteristics—such as broad-range, tunable GQD-loading contents and particle sizes, extremely low cytotoxicity in normal and cancer cells, and highly sensitive pH-responsiveness and rapid acid-triggered fluorescent behavior under aqueous acidic conditions. We show these features are conferred by self-aggregation of the GQD within the nanoparticles and subsequent aggregation-induced fluorescence of GQD after disassembly of the nanoparticles and dissociation of the D-A interactions under acidic conditions. Importantly, in vitro fluorescence imaging experiments clearly demonstrated the WPT/GQD nanoparticles were gradually taken up into normal and cancer cells in vitro. Selective formation of GQD aggregates subsequently occurred in the acidic microenvironment of the cancer cells and the interior of the cancer cells exhibited strong blue fluorescence; these phenomena did not occur in normal cells. In contrast, pristine WPT and GQD did not exhibit cellular microenvironment-triggered fluorescence transitions in cancer or normal cell lines. Therefore, this newly discovered water-soluble heterojunction system may represent a strongly fluorescent highly pH-sensitive bioprobe for rapid detection of cancer cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Conjugated polymer
Donor-acceptor energy transfer
Graphene quantum dot
Heterojunction nanoparticles
Selective cancer detection

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: PL spectra (excitation at 340 nm) of different concentrations of (a) WPT, (b) GQD, and (d) WPT/GQD complexes in water at 25 °C. The inset photographs show the fluorescence characteristics of various concentrations of (a) WPT, (b) GQD, and (c) WPT/GQD complexes in water at 25 °C under natural light and long-wavelength UV light.

(a) DLS analysis of WPT, GQD, and 0.1/0.5 WPT/GQD complexes in water at 25 °C. (b) Hydrodynamic diameter and GQD-loading content as a function of GQD additive concentration for WPT/GQD complexes in water. AFM topography images of spin-coated (c) WPT and (d) WPT/GQD nanoparticles recorded at 25 °C.

(a) 1H NMR spectra of WPT, GQD, and 0.1/0.5 WPT/GQD complexes in D2O recorded at 25 °C. (b) WAXS results for WPT, GQD, and 0.1/0.5 WPT/GQD complexes at 25 °C after lyophilization. (c) XPS spectra of the C1s region of WPT, GQD, and 0.1/0.5 WPT/GQD complexes at 25 °C.

PL spectra (excitation at 340 nm) of (a) WPT, (b) GQD, and (c) 0.1/0.5 WPT/GQD complexes in water at different pH values (pH 7.4, 6.2, and 1.0) at 25 °C. (c) The inset photograph shows the blue fluorescence emission of 0.1/0.5 WPT/GQD complexes in water at pH 1.0 under long-wavelength UV light. (d) Suggested possible mechanism for the disassembly process of WPT/GQD nanoparticles in aqueous acidic solution, which subsequently promotes the formation of GQD aggregates with high-intensity blue fluorescence emission. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
CLSM images of (a) NIH/3T3 and (b) HeLa cells cultured with 0.1/0.5 WPT/GQD nanoparticles at 37 °C for 1, 6, 16, or 24 h. The white scale bars in each image represent 20 μm.

(a) Graphical representation of the self-assembly of WPT/GQD complexes in water and their fluorescence response under aqueous acidic conditions. (b) The dashed frame illustrates D-A energy transfer from WPT to GQD.

head.ce:title: Water-soluble graphene quantum dot‐based polymer nanoparticles with internal donor/acceptor heterojunctions for efficient and selective detection of cancer cells

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Natural Science, Faculty of Mathematics and Natural Science, Universitas Negeri Surabaya, Surabaya 60231, Indonesia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Natural Science, Faculty of Mathematics and Natural Science, Universitas Negeri Surabaya

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Natural Science, Faculty of Mathematics and Natural Science, Universitas Negeri Surabaya, Surabaya 60231, Indonesia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Water-soluble GQD‐based heterojunction polymer nanoparticles exhibit highly sensitive pH-responsiveness and rapid acid-triggered fluorescent behavior under aqueous acidic conditions and can selectively induce strong blue fluorescence in cancer cells, thus may potentially contribute to the rapid detection of cancer cells.

We present a new, insightful donor–acceptor (D-A) energy transfer-based strategy for the preparation and development of water-soluble multifunctional pH-responsive heterojunction nanoparticles. Hydrophilic tertiary amine-grafted polythiophene (WPT) as a donor and blue fluorescent graphene quantum dots (GQD) as an acceptor spontaneously form co-assembled nanoparticles that function as a highly pH-sensitive and efficient biosensor appropriate for the detection of cancer cells. These WPT/GQD nanoparticles exhibit a number of unique physical characteristics—such as broad-range, tunable GQD-loading contents and particle sizes, extremely low cytotoxicity in normal and cancer cells, and highly sensitive pH-responsiveness and rapid acid-triggered fluorescent behavior under aqueous acidic conditions. We show these features are conferred by self-aggregation of the GQD within the nanoparticles and subsequent aggregation-induced fluorescence of GQD after disassembly of the nanoparticles and dissociation of the D-A interactions under acidic conditions. Importantly, in vitro fluorescence imaging experiments clearly demonstrated the WPT/GQD nanoparticles were gradually taken up into normal and cancer cells in vitro. Selective formation of GQD aggregates subsequently occurred in the acidic microenvironment of the cancer cells and the interior of the cancer cells exhibited strong blue fluorescence; these phenomena did not occur in normal cells. In contrast, pristine WPT and GQD did not exhibit cellular microenvironment-triggered fluorescence transitions in cancer or normal cell lines. Therefore, this newly discovered water-soluble heterojunction system may represent a strongly fluorescent highly pH-sensitive bioprobe for rapid detection of cancer cells.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Quantum dots (QD) have attracted enormous enthusiasm as viable emitters for lighting and displays due to their distinctive optoelectronic features, such as tunable emission wavelengths, color purity, intense fluorescence efficiency, and low-cost [1,2]. Among the various QD nanomaterials, graphene quantum dots (GQD), a novel form of zero-dimensional QD, have received considerable attention from both academia and industry as their exceptional structural features include single-layer or a few-layer thicknesses and a very small (less than10 nm) lateral size [3,4]. GQD typically exhibit the unique characteristics of two-dimensional graphene nanosheets. Moreover, GQD also exhibit various other advantages—including superior optical and electronic properties, excellent solubility in aqueous solutions, stable fluorescence emission with colors ranging from blue to yellow, low cytotoxicity, good biocompatibility, and extraordinary resistance to photo-bleaching compared with traditional QD or organic dyes—thus have huge biomedical potential as drug carriers and for diagnosis of diseases, bioimaging, and biosensing [5–10]. Notably, GQD possess a number of additional active and intriguing characteristics due to their level of surface oxidation, surface charge density, and the doping status of heteroatoms or covalent linkages with their functional polymers, which can significantly alter their interactions with the environment and other biomolecules [11–15]. Recently, exciting progress demonstrated that the graphene surface of GQD can form covalent bonds with antibodies, proteins, nucleic acids, and biopolymers, which was used to endow GQD with highly specific and biomarker ability for multiple types of cancer [16–18]. The up-conversion photoluminescence (UCPL) properties of GQD are also attractive for cancer cell bioimaging and have been extensively tested in human cervical cancer HeLa cells [19–21]. Nevertheless, despite their significant advantages for bioimaging, a number of obstacles limit the use of GQD in biomedical applications, such as the critical demand for effective synthesis and separation methods and tunable fluorescent colors with high quantum efficiency, as well as strategies to improve their solubility and alleviate the effects of dilution in aqueous solutions [22,23]. Therefore, the development of an efficient route to enhance the long-term structural stability and fluorescence properties of GQD in aqueous solutions is not only highly desirable, but also an urgent challenge.

Over the past few years, numerous research groups have achieved significant progress in the design and preparation of novel water-soluble conjugated polymers (WCP) that readily dissolve in environmentally friendly solvents (e.g., water or alcohols) to meet the standards for green organic electronic devices [24–26]. Generally, WCP are composed of two components: a π-conjugated polymer backbone, which affects the optical properties of the WCP (such as absorption, emission, and quantum yield), and charged or hydrophilic side-chains that confer the ability to dissolve in aqueous solution and facilitate interactions with water molecules and biosubstances in aqueous solution [27–30]. Moreover, when applied to organic electronic devices via a simple spin-coating process, WCP-based devices exhibit excellent optoelectronic performance with a low operating voltage and high energy conversion efficiency [31,32]. The hydrophobic backbone significantly affects the amphiphilic properties and enables WCP to self-assemble into well-controlled hierarchical nanostructures with multifunctional features in aqueous solution [33–35]. Thus, the spontaneous self-assembly behavior of WCP in aqueous solution can efficiently prevent issues related to hydrophobic interaction-induced dynamic aggregation and precipitation, and thereby improves their structural stability and the balance between hydrophilic and hydrophobic characteristics [36–38]. Most importantly, electron-rich WCP can undergo complimentary π-conjugated electron donor–acceptor (D-A) charge transfer with electron-deficient carbon-based nanomaterials, which leads to the formation of functional complexes with desirable structural stability and electronic properties [39,40]. Thus, we can reasonably hypothesize that the combination of GQD and WCP may lead to the formation of stable complexes in water via D-A energy transfer between GQD and WCP, and thereby possibly confer enhanced structural stability and stimuli-responsive properties that are potentially suitable for various biomedical applications.

In previous studies, we successfully obtained high-performance electrospun nanofibers with high electrical conductivity at an ultra-low carbon nanotube (CNT) content by directly blending diaminopyridine-functionalized polythiophene with CNT due to the efficient D-A interactions between conjugated polythiophene and CNT [41]. In addition, we also developed water-soluble hybrid polymer micelles that encapsulate hydrophobic fullerene into self-assembled water-soluble polythiophene nanostructures via D-A energy transfer [42]. The resulting hybrid micelles were employed in a solution-processed single-layer device, which showed a higher electrical conductivity than control polythiophene and fullerene devices. Drawing inspiration from these findings, we further extended this concept to the development of water-soluble polytiophene/GQD heterojunction composites for biosensor and bioimaging applications. Herein, we successfully synthesized blue fluorescent GQD and water-soluble tertiary amine-grafted polyolythiohene (WPT). Owing to the efficient D-A energy transfer between WPT and GQD, WPT can form stable, water-soluble, non-covalent complexes with GQD that subsequently further assemble into spherical heterojunction nanoparticles in water (Scheme 1 ). These nanoparticles possess a number of unique physical characteristics, such as wide-range tunable GQD loading content, well-controlled particle sizes, extremely low cytotoxicity towards normal and cancer cells, rapid pH-responsive properties, and pH-triggered fluorescence emission in aqueous acidic environments. Importantly, WPT/GQD nanoparticles were gradually internalized by normal and cancer cells in culture and exhibited selective fluorescence properties in cancer cells, whereas pristine WPT and GQD did not undergo a cell microenvironment-triggered fluorescence transition in either cancer or normal cells. This observation could be attributed to programmed disassembly of the WPT/GQD nanoparticles in the acidic microenvironment of cancer cells, which would subsequently promote the formation of GQD aggregates inside the cells and induce strong blue fluorescence behavior. As far as we are aware, there are no previous reports of water-soluble heterojunction nanoparticles based on D-A energy transfer between WPT and GQD that enable manipulation of the co-assembly and fluorescence properties and achieve a highly pH-sensitive fluorescent response that enables the detection of cancer cells. Therefore, this newly discovered heterojunction system holds enormous potential for biosensor and bioimaging applications.
Details regarding synthetic procedures, experimental measurements, in vitro studies and instrumentation used in this study are given in the Supplementary Information .

Scheme 1 presents the molecular structure of π-complementary D-A materials with fluorescence resonance energy transfer behavior. To develop a facile and efficient route for the synthesis of water-soluble tertiary amine-grafted oligomeric polythiophene (WPT), a two-step reaction process was employed, as illustrated in Scheme S1 (see Supporting Information). In the first step, the monomer tertiary amine-containing thiophene was prepared by a direct condensation reaction between N,N-dimethyl-1,3‑diaminopropane and ethyl 3-thiopheneacetate. Subsequently, the monomer was further reacted by oxidative coupling polymerization with ferric chloride under cold conditions to form the WPT polymer. The chemical structure of WPT is shown in Scheme 1a. After purification by dialysis against deionized water, the resulting WPT was obtained at nearly 50 % yield, and exhibited the desired polymeric structure and low molecular weight of approximately 3000 (corresponding to an average of 12 repeat units), as confirmed by nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopy (NMR) and mass spectrometry (Figs. S1–S4 and Table S1). Due to efficient D-A energy transfer from WPT as a donor to carbon-based GQD as an acceptor [43–45], blue fluorescent GQD was obtained by a one-pot hydrothermal method via pyrolysis of citric acid [46–48]. The obtained GQD had high water solubility and exhibited spherical morphologies and diameters ranging from 20 nm to 50 nm, as observed by scanning electron microscopy and transmission electron microscopy (Fig. S5). Before exploring the D-A energy transfer behavior of the WPT/GQD complexes, we studied the fluorescence characteristics of WPT and GQD in aqueous solution to understand the self-assembly behavior and optophysical transition of WPT/GQD in aqueous solution and guide the development of functional heterostructure composites based on D-A energy transfer. Thus, according to absorption peaks observed in the ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectra of WPT and GQD in aqueous solution, photoluminescence (PL) spectra were obtained under excitation at 340 nm (Fig. S6). As shown in Fig. 1 a, the maximum fluorescence wavelength of WPT was centered at 562 nm; the intensity of this peak gradually increased with the concentration of WPT and reached a maximum at 0.1 mg/mL WPT; WPT emitted strong yellow fluorescence (see the insert digital photographs of Fig. 1a). Further increasing the concentration of the aqueous WPT solutions from 0.1 to 3 mg/mL led to a gradual decrease in the fluorescence intensity; the fluorescence intensity of aqueous 3 mg/mL WPT was almost undetectable. Thus, the 0.1 mg/mL WPT solution exhibited the optimal optical properties and highest fluorescence intensity, possibly due to a low extent of polymer chain entanglement or aggregation in water resulting in a significantly higher fluorescent signal. Thus, 0.1 mg/mL WPT in water was chosen for all further studies of the formation of WPT/GQD complexes. In contrast to the fluorescence behavior of WPT in water, GQD in water exhibited a maximum fluorescence peak centered at 423 nm, accompanied by a weak shoulder peak at 548 nm. The overall fluorescence intensity gradually increased with the concentration of GQD from 0.01 to 3 mg/mL; GQD emitted intrinsic blue fluorescence (see the insert digital photographs of Fig. 1b). These results indicate that the fluorescence intensity of GQD is directly proportional to its concentration in water. It is especially noteworthy that GQD exhibited almost no fluorescence signal at concentrations less than 0.1 mg/mL, which suggests that GQD formed isolated nanoparticles within the dilute solution, and the lack of a sufficient number of nanoparticles to enhance the frequency of the collisions between particles led to an almost complete loss of intrinsic fluorescent features [22]. In other words, GQD has a high tendency to form large aggregates at high concentrations, which improves its structural stability and fluorescence behavior in water. Taken together, these interesting observations prompted us to explore whether the combination of WPT and GQD via complementary D-A interactions affects the self-assembly behavior and optophysical characteristics of WPT/GQD complexes in aqueous medium.

Thus, we investigated the extent of D-A energy transfer and fluorescence transformation behavior of WPT/GQD complexes after adding 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 0.8, or 1.0 mg/mL GQD into 0.1 mg/mL WPT in water; the resulting mixed WPT/GQD complexes are hereinafter individually referred to as 0.1/0.1, 0.1/0.2, 0.1/0.5, 0.1/0.8, and 0.1/1.0 WPT/GQD. The macroscopic phenomena of the aqueous WPT/GQD solutions were initially examined by digital photography and PL spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 1c, the color of the WPT/GQD solutions gradually changed from yellow to colorless as the content of GQD in WPT solution increased from 0.1 to 0.5 mg/mL. Moreover, the strong bright yellow fluorescence of WPT observed under broadband long-wave ultraviolet radiation became gradually quenched and completely disappeared at 0.1/0.5 WPT/GQD. These results suggest that introduction of GQD into WPT solution remarkably alters the fluorescence properties of WPT: fluorescence quenching occurs linearly as the concentration of GQD increases due to efficient D-A energy transfer between WPT and GQD, which results in the formation of stable heterojunction complex structures in water. A similar fluorescence quenching phenomenon was observed in the PL measurements. As shown in Fig. 1d, the PL spectra obtained at 25 °C indicated the fluorescence intensity of WPT at 562 nm progressively decreased as the content of GQD increased. Notably, the addition of 0.5 mg/mL GQD into 0.1 mg/mL WPT solution led to complete quenching of fluorescence, indicating that the blend ratio of the 0.1/0.5 WPT/GQD complex was the optimal composition to achieve complete D-A energy transfer from WPT to GQD. Furthermore, these results imply that the presence of GQD in the WPT solution continuously converts the highly fluorescent π-conjugated polymer nanostructures into non-fluorescent hybrid heterostructures. These intriguing findings piqued our curiosity to further explore the self-assembly behavior and structural morphology of WPT/GQD complexes in water.

Owing to the complementary D-A energy transfer between WPT and GQD, we investigated the physical effects of WPT on GQD in water in terms of the encapsulation ability, co-assembled structures, and morphological characteristics of the complexes. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the average diameters of 0.1 mg/mL WPT and 0.5 mg/mL GQD in water were 74 ± 13 nm and 41 ± 9 nm, respectively, which confirms WPT and GQD can spontaneously form nanosized objects in water. After the encapsulation process and purification by dialysis, the average diameter of the WPT/GQD complexes increased gradually from 173 ± 46 nm (zeta-potential ζ value = −20.26 ± 1.68 mV) to 368 ± 29 nm (ζ value = 12.27 ± 3.07 mV) as the content of GQD increased from 0.1 to 1.0 mg/mL, suggesting that a gradual increase in hydrodynamic diameter is required to accommodate more GQD within the complex structure to achieve a higher loading capacity of GQD (Fig. 2a and 2b). The resulting complexes had a maximum GQD-loading content of around 39 % as determined by as PL spectroscopy, thus the amount of GQD loaded into the WPT/GQD complexes could be readily adjusted and tailored (Fig. 2b). These results also revealed that the D-A interactions within the WPT/GQD complexes efficiently enhance their structural stability and thus increase the efficiency of the physical entrapment of hydrophilic GQD, as the fluorescence characteristics of WPT could be quenched by GQD through a D-A energy transfer mechanism, as illustrated in Scheme 1b. In order to confirm the DLS measurements of the average diameter of the GQD-loaded nanoobjects, we assessed the morphology and self-assembled structures of pristine WPT and GQD-loaded WPT complexes using tapping mode AFM. As shown in Fig. 2c and 2d, both materials exhibited spherical-like morphologies with different particle sizes. The diameter of WPT nanoparticles ranged from 30 to 40 nm, whereas the 0.1/1.0 WPT/GQD complexes exhibited particle sizes larger than 300 nm with a uniform size distribution, in good agreement with the DLS data (Fig. 2a). This result further confirmed that GQD was successfully entrapped into the self-assembled nanoparticles via the D-A interactions, which promote the formation of spherical-like GQD-containing WPT/GQD nanoparticles with wide-range tunable GQD-loading capacity.

After characterizing the self-assembly behavior and morphological characteristics of the WPT/GQD complexes in water, we investigated the bonding properties of the D-A interactions in the WPT/GQD complexes in deuterium oxide (D2O) by proton 1H NMR spectroscopy. When a WPT/GQD complex was prepared by mixing WPT (0.1 mg/mL) and GQD (0.5 mg/mL) in D2O, 1H NMR indicated that the characteristic protons of GQD at 2.58 and 2.51 ppm (signals 1 and 2) shifted significantly downfield to 3.82 and 3.58 ppm (signals 1′ and 2′), respectively (Fig. 3 a). This phenomenon is possibly due to the formation of hydrogen bonding interactions between the carboxylic acid moieties of GQD and the pendant amide groups of WPT to form stable WPT/GQD complexes; these hydrogen bonding interactions promote stable energy transfer from WPT to GQD and eventually result in fluorescence quenching. In addition, the 1H NMR results also suggest that the presence of intramolecular hydrogen bonds within the WPT/GQD nanoparticles may be a critical factor required for achievement of a high GQD loading content and the improved structural stability of GQD-loaded WPT nanoparticles in water. Synchrotron wide-angle X-ray scattering (WAXS) was subsequently carried out at 25 °C to analyze the phase behavior and microstructure of WPT/GQD complexes after the removal of water by lyophilization. As shown in Fig. 3b, the WAXS profile of WPT revealed two broad scattering peaks centered at q = 0.25 Å−1 (d = 2.51 nm) and q = 1.59 Å−1 (d = 0.39 nm). The long period peak at q = 0.25 Å−1 suggests that the pendant side-groups along the polymer backbone increase the intermolecular distance between the main polymer chains and reduce polymer chain entanglement [49,50]. This result also indicates that the formation of intermolecular packing between pendent side chains on the d-spacing of WPT may be attributed to the formation of hydrogen bonds between the amine and carbonyl groups in the amide repeat unit, and these bonds enhance the stability of the self-assembled structures. The other peak at q = 1.59 Å−1 belongs to the intra- and intermolecular amorphous regions, indicating that WPT behaves as a typical amorphous polymer [51]. In contrast, the WAXS profile of GQD exhibited several characteristic crystalline peaks, including three sharp low-q peaks at q = 0.62, 0.66, and 0.77 Å−1, which reveals that GQD possesses a certain degree of crystalline characteristics and exhibits a variety of crystal packing arrangements [52]. Interestingly, for the 0.1/0.5 WPT/GQD complexes, incorporation of GQD into WPT led to the disappearance of the long period peaks of WPT and GQD at q = 0.25, 0.62, 0.66, and 0.77 Å−1, respectively, indicating that the self-complementary hydrogen bonding interactions and ordered packed structures of WPT and GQD were completely disassembled in the binary blend of WPT with GQD. Instead, the WAXS pattern of the WPT/GQD complexes contained three new scattering peaks at q = 0.83 Å−1 (d = 0.76 nm), 1.89 Å−1 (d = 0.33 nm), and 2.44 Å−1 (d = 0.26 nm), suggesting that stacks of the complexes are held together through π–π interactions between WPT and GQD [53–55]. These results also further indicate that the complementary interaction of WPT with GQD via aromatic–aromatic or π–π stacking interactions is more energetically favorable than the self-complementary secondary interactions between either WPT or GQD molecules, and these energetically favorable interactions promote efficient D-A energy transfer from WPT to GQD and result in formation of stable heterojunction nanoparticles in water.

In order to confirm these results, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was employed to further explore the specific interactions between WPT and GQD in the solid state at 25 °C. After removing the water by lyophilization, the dried samples were assessed by XPS. As presented in Fig. 3c, the high resolution C1s spectra of pristine WCP and GQD could be deconvoluted into four and five individual peaks, respectively, namely CC, CC, CO, CN, and CO peaks [56,57]. Surprisingly, the CN and CO peaks of the C1s spectrum of the 0.1/0.5 WPT/GQD complex were shifted toward significantly higher binding energies compared to the peaks of pristine WPT and GQD, indicating the existence of specific hydrogen bonding interactions between the amide moieties of WPT and the carboxylic acid groups on the surface of GQD [58,59]. These results demonstrate that WPT/GQD complexes form due to noncovalent intermolecular locking via intermolecular hydrogen bonds between WPT and GQD, which further promote stable and efficient D-A energy transfer from WPT to GQD and subsequent co-assembly of non-fluorescent nanoparticles in water. These XPS results are also in good agreement with the 1H NMR and WAXS observations in Fig. 3a and 3b. Collectively, based on the findings above, the complementary hydrogen bonds within the WPT/GQD system can be efficiently used to control the D-A energy-transfer route from WPT to GQD, which is beneficial and desirable for widely tuning the structural and optical properties of WPT/GQD nanoparticles to achieve tailorable particle sizes, GQD loading contents, and fluorescence characteristics.

Ideally, a heterojunction composite must possess high structural stability in normal physiological environments and exhibit rapid responses under slightly acidic conditions to ensure the reliability and responsiveness of the material for potential applications in different fields of biosensing, bioimaging and biomedicine [60,61]. Therefore, we further explored the fluorescence stability of pristine WPT, GQD, and 0.1/0.5 WPT/GQD nanoparticles in aqueous solutions at different pH values (pH 7.4, 6.1, and 1.0); dilute hydrochloric acid (HCl) was used to adjust the pH of the aqueous solutions, then the samples were incubated for 24 h at 25 °C and measured by PL. As shown in Fig. 4 a and 4b, at low concentrations (0.1 mg/mL), WPT and GQD in water exhibited gradual decreases in fluorescence intensity as the pH decreased from 7.4 to 1.0, while the characteristic fluorescence peak of WPT significantly redshifted from 562 nm to 574 nm, indicating that both materials exhibit low structural stability in acidic aqueous environments. These observations are probably due to protonation of the tertiary amine moieties of WPT by HCl, which further increases the hydrophilic properties of the pendant group of WPT and causes a redshift in the fluorescence peak of the conjugated backbone of WPT, as well as a gradual reduction in the fluorescence intensity of this peak. Surprisingly, PL spectra revealed that a fluorescence peak suddenly appeared at 446 nm for the 0.1/0.5 WPT/GQD complexes as the pH was decreased from 7.4 to 6.2 (Fig. 4c); this fluorescence peak was markedly red-shifted (from 423 nm to 446 nm) compared to that of pristine GQD (Fig. 1b and 4b). When the pH was further decreased to 6.2 and 1.0, the WPT/GQD fluorescence peak at 446 nm further increased in intensity from 450 to 932 a.u. and the resulting solution exhibited obvious blue emission under broadband long-wave ultraviolet irradiation (Fig. 4c and the middle inset photographs). The linear increase in the fluorescence intensity of the WPT/GQD complexes and red-shift in their fluorescence peak as the pH gradually increased could mainly be attributed to gradual protonation of the tertiary amine moieties of WPT under acidic environments, which subsequently leads to disassembly of the complexes. In addition, disassembly of the complexes and dissociation of the D-A interactions increases the tendency of GQD within the nanoparticles to form non-covalent self-aggregates (rather than interact with WPT) at acidic pH. This results in the apparent red shift in emission and increase in fluorescence intensity, even if the loss occurs on the region encapsulated and protected by WPT, i.e., the random coils formed by WPT chains in acidic medium (Fig. 4d). In other words, acidic conditions promote self-aggregation and increase the structural stability of the GQD within the nanoparticles and subsequently induce aggregation-induced fluorescence of GQD after dissociation of the nanoparticles under acidic conditions. However, under strong acidic conditions (pH 1.0), the intensity of the fluorescence peak of the WPT/GQD complexes reduced to half after 10 days and completely disappeared after one month, as monitored by PL at 25 °C. This result indicates time-dependent fluorescence decay occurs due to the dynamic nature of the aggregated structures, due to gradual dissociation of the GQD aggregates over time under acidic conditions. The combination of the unique D-A interactions, highly sensitive pH-responsiveness, and acid-triggered fluorescence behavior of the WPT/GQD nanoparticles are potentially attractive and useful features that are rarely found in conventional water-soluble heterojunction materials [62,63]. Therefore, WPT/GQD complexes may represent a multifunctional composite system that holds potential for biomedical imaging and sensing as a rapid, facile, and effective tool that efficiently detects and responds accurately in specific complex cellular environments.

Cancer cells have a slightly lower pH (intracellular environment of 7.4, and pH ranges of 6.7–7.1 in extracellular matrix, 6.5 in early endosomes, 5.0 in late endosomes and 4.0–5.0 in lysosomes) than normal cells (intracellular environment of 7.4 and 7.2 in extracellular matrix) [64–67]. Therefore, the acidic environment-induced fluorescence emission of WPT/GQD nanoparticles inspired us to further assess their biocompatibility and fluorescence responses in human cervical cancer HeLa cells and human normal fibroblast NIH/3T3 cells using the colorimetric MTT assay [3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5‑diphenyl tetrazolium bromide] and confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM), respectively. These in vitro studies were performed to confirm the pH-responsiveness and acid-triggered fluorescence of WPT/GQD nanoparticles in complex cellular environments. After co-culture for 24 h at 37 °C and pH 7.4, 0.1–50 μg/mL pristine WPT, GQD, or 0.1/0.5 WPT/GQD nanoparticles exerted no significant adverse or toxic effects against NIH/3T3 or HeLa cell lines (Figs. S7 and S8), indicating that these materials are biocompatible with both normal and cancer cells. Next, we visually evaluated the fluorescence behavior, cellular uptake, and intracellular trafficking of WPT/GQD nanoparticles in NIH/3T3 and HeLa cells by CLSM. Fluorescently-labeled phalloidin emits neon-green fluorescence and was used to stain filamentous actin and cytoskeletal structures; 4′,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI) is a blue fluorescent staining reagent used to stain cell nuclei; WPT and GQD display red and blue fluorescence, respectively. As indicated in Figs. S9 and S10, after 1, 6, 16, or 24 h of co-culture, the characteristic fluorescent signals of WPT or GQD were clearly observed in both NIH/3T3 and HeLa cells. In addition, the fluorescence intensities gradually increased with the culture time, suggesting that WPT and GQD were progressively internalized by the NIH/3T3 and HeLa cells and did not exhibit any pH-responsive behavior or changes in fluorescence, even after 24 h culture. In contrast to pristine WPT and GQD, NIH/3T3 cells cultured with 0.1/0.5 WPT/GQD nanoparticles for 24 h only showed very faint blue GQD fluorescence in the cytoskeleton and the characteristic red fluorescence of WPT was not observed. This result indicates that the D-A interactions within the WPT/GQD nanoparticles remained stable and thus maintained low fluorescence or a non-fluorescent state in NIH/3T3 cells (Fig. 5 a). In contrast, HeLa cells cultured with 0.1/0.5 WPT/GQD nanoparticles displayed faint blue fluorescence in the cytoskeleton after only 1 h, and this fluorescence signal gradually increased in intensity and progressively moved into the interior of the cells as the culture time increased from 1 h to 24 h (Fig. 5b). These observations reveal that WPT/GQD nanoparticles underwent progressive cellular uptake into HeLa cells and that the WPT/GQD nanoparticles gradually disassembled in the acidic microenvironment of the endosomes/lysosomes in HeLa cells, which ultimately promoted the formation of GQD aggregates with strong blue fluorescent emission in the intracellular compartments of HeLa cells. Notably, no red fluorescence was observed within the nuclei of HeLa cells incubated with 0.1/0.5 WPT/GQD nanoparticles for 24 h, which suggests that a high affinity interaction (or specific electrostatic interaction) between the WPT/GQD nanoparticles and the cell surface promotes efficient transport of the nanoparticles from the plasma membrane into the interior of the cell, which subsequently facilitates disassembly of the nanoparticles in the highly acidic endosomes and lysosomes and promotes protonation of free WPT in the interior of the HeLa cells, resulting in the formation of random-coil WPT chains and complete quenching of its red fluorescence. These observations are in good agreement with our PL results (Fig. 4) and further demonstrate that WPT/GQD nanoparticles exhibit highly sensitive pH-triggered fluorescence emission in the intracellular environment of cancer cells, mainly due to destruction of the self-assembled nanoparticles and the subsequent formation of GQD aggregates in the acidic microenvironment of the cancer cells [64–67]. In contrast, WPT/GQD nanoparticles exhibited almost no fluorescence response in normal NIH/3T3 cells. Thus, these findings clearly demonstrate that WPT/GQD nanoparticles may represent a sensitive, safe, and efficient bioprobe that could be used for the rapid detection of cancer cells and could potentially be combined with targeted drugs to enable highly efficient cancer therapy.

In summary, we report the development of the first multifunctional water-soluble heterojunction composite that hold potentials as a highly sensitive pH-responsive fluorescent biosensor suitable for the rapid and efficient detection of cancer cells. A new WPT consisting of pendant tertiary amine groups and a polythiophene backbone was successfully produced via a novel two-step synthetic route and can rapidly self-assemble in water into nearly spherical nanostructures that possess unique optical and fluorescence properties. Owing to the existence of efficient D-A energy transfer between WPT and GQD, WPT can generate stable water-soluble non-covalent complexes with GQD, which further organize into spherical WPT/GQD heterojunction nanoparticles in water with a wide-range tunable GQD-loading content, well-controlled particle sizes, and good biocompatibility towards normal and cancer cells. In addition, the resulting WPT/GQD nanoparticles exhibit highly sensitive pH-responsiveness and rapid acid-triggered fluorescent behavior under aqueous acidic conditions. These phenomena can be attributed to the fact that acidic conditions promote aggregation of the GQD within the nanoparticles, as aggregation-induced fluorescence of GQD occurs after disassembly of the nanoparticles and dissociation of the D-A interactions under acidic conditions. The combination of these intriguing and unique characteristics is potentially attractive and highly desirable, but rarely present in existing water-soluble heterojunction material systems. Fluorescence confocal microscopy demonstrated that pristine WPT and GQD were gradually internalized and exhibited persistent fluorescence in NIH/3T3 and HeLa cells. However, importantly, the WPT/GQD nanoparticles were gradually internalized into HeLa cancer cells and programmed disassembly of the nanoparticles occurred in the acidic microenvironment of the HeLa cells, which induced aggregation of strongly blue fluorescent GQD in the interior of the cells. In contrast, this pH-responsive behavior and acidic microenvironment-triggered fluorescence were not observed in normal NIH/3T3 cells. Thus, the D-A interactions within the self-assembled WPT/GQD nanoparticles significantly enhance the selective cellular uptake of nanoparticles by cancer cells and confer rapid, highly pH-responsive fluorescence behavior in cancer cells. Overall, this newly discovered system represents a potentially safe and efficient fluorescent pH-sensitive bioprobe for rapid detection of cancer cells and could be potentially combined with anticancer drugs to increase the efficacy of cancer chemotherapy.

Yi-Hsuan Chang: Data curation, Investigation, Methodology. Wei-Hung Chiang: Investigation, Resources. Fasih Bintang Ilhami:Investigation, Validation. Cheng-Yu Tsai: Investigation, Validation. Sin-Yu Huang: Investigation, Validation. Chih-Chia Cheng: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Methodology, Resources, Supervision, Visualization, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported financially by the National Science and Technology Council, Taiwan (contract no. NSTC 110-2221-E-011-003-MY3).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2023.01.104.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
The full information of Experimental Section and results of NMR, Mass, Elemental Analysis, MALDI-TOF MS, SEM, TEM, UV-vis, MTT assay and CLSM images are presented in Supporting Information.
